analysis.note: Adjective modifying caitasaiḥ; rendered as a technical class, “universal/all-pervading.”

verification.detail: compound stem sarvatraga as Pum/Trtiya/Bahu: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Kta-participial adjective from vi-ni-√yam, used here as a class adjective with caitasaiḥ; translated “fixed/determinate.”
Adjective modifying caitasaiḥ; in this context “wholesome/good.”
Feminine nominative singular pronoun, subject of saṃprayuktā and trivedanā; antecedent is context-dependent, here translated as “that [cognition].”
Past passive participial adjective from saṃ-pra-√yuj, agreeing with asau; governs the instrumental groups: “associated/joined with.”
Instrumental plural governed by saṃprayuktā; translated in the Buddhist technical sense “afflictions.”
Instrumental plural governed by saṃprayuktā; “secondary afflictions/lesser afflictions.”
Predicate adjective agreeing with asau, not a separate plural noun phrase.

verification.detail: compound stem trivedanā as Stri/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: The nominative feminine singular forms asau, saṃprayuktā, and trivedanā are construed together: “that [cognition] is associated … and has three feelings.”
options_considered: trivedanā as an independent noun phrase, “three feelings”
saṃprayuktā as governing only the immediately preceding caitasaiḥ
justifications.chosen: asau as feminine subject; saṃprayuktā and trivedanā as agreeing predicates

evidence.translation: Grammatical basis: asau is nominative feminine singular; saṃprayuktā and trivedanā are also nominative feminine singular, so they naturally agree as predicates of the same subject.

justifications.decision: The first three instrumental plurals are taken as class-adjectives of caitasaiḥ: “mental factors that are universal, fixed/determinate, and wholesome.”
options_considered: three separate instrumental substantives plus caitasaiḥ
caitasaiḥ as merely adjectival, “mental,” with no implied substantive
justifications.chosen: sarvatragaiḥ, viniyataiḥ, and kuśalaiḥ as adjectives qualifying caitasaiḥ

evidence.translation: Grammatical basis: sarvatragaiḥ, viniyataiḥ, kuśalaiḥ, and caitasaiḥ are all masculine instrumental plural and stand as a compact pre-nominal cluster.

justifications.decision: The word is treated as an instrumental plural adjective from the participial stem viniyata, not as a finite or root form; the translation keeps a cautious lexical sense from vi-ni-√yam, “restrain, regulate.”
options_considered: “restraining” as an active present participial sense
a fully specified technical list not recoverable from grammar alone

evidence.translation: Lexical/grammatical basis: MW gives vi-ni-√yam as “restrain, check, control, regulate”; viniyata is therefore a kta-adjective meaning “restrained, regulated, fixed,” here agreeing with caitasaiḥ.

justifications.decision: tathā is translated “likewise/also,” extending the association construction: “likewise with afflictions and secondary afflictions.”
options_considered: tathā modifying trivedanā: “three feelings in that way”
tathā beginning a new independent clause
kleśair upakleśaiḥ as instruments of an implied verb other than saṃprayuktā
justifications.chosen: tathā links the kleśa/upakleśa instrumentals to the same predicate saṃprayuktā

evidence.translation: Grammatical basis: kleśaiḥ and upakleśaiḥ are instrumental plurals like caitasaiḥ and therefore fit the same construction with saṃprayuktā; tathā functions as a connective adverb.

justifications.decision: Because trivedanā is feminine nominative singular and agrees with asau, it is taken as a compound predicate adjective rather than as a simple plural noun phrase.
options_considered: “three feelings” as a separate nominative plural
dvigu collective “a triad of feelings” as the subject
“threefold feeling” as an abstract noun unrelated to asau
justifications.chosen: trivedanā as a bahuvrīhi adjective, “having three feelings”

evidence.translation: Grammatical basis: tri + vedanā in a singular feminine compound can qualify a feminine singular subject; the bahuvrīhi vigraha is “she/it whose feelings are three.”

one_shot_delta: A purely word-for-word rendering might translate caitasaiḥ only as “mental ones,” missing its likely substantival force, “mental factors.”
It might take trivedanā as a separate phrase, “three feelings,” instead of an adjective of asau: “having three kinds of feeling.”
It might fail to carry the force of saṃprayuktā over both instrumental groups, thereby disconnecting kleśaiḥ and upakleśaiḥ from “associated with.”
Without commentary, the precise doctrinal contents of the classes sarvatraga, viniyata, kuśala, kleśa, and upakleśa cannot be fixed from this verse alone.
analyzer_disagreements: viniyataiḥ: ByT5 lemmatizes as viniyam, but the inflected verse-word is a subanta from the kta-adjectival stem viniyata; features as masculine instrumental plural are retained.

saṃprayuktā: ByT5 gives a participial/verbal lemma saṃprayuj, but the apparatus requires the nominal stem for subanta analysis, so lemma is recorded as saṃprayukta; feminine nominative singular features are retained.
trivedanā: ByT5 segments tri + vedanā; I record the printed word as a single bahuvrīhi compound stem trivedanā, feminine nominative singular, because it agrees predicatively with asau.
open_questions: No commentary was supplied for this ablation run; the exact antecedent of asau is inferred only as a feminine singular item from the preceding context, here rendered “that [cognition].”
The exact technical sense and list denoted by viniyata are not fixed by the dictionary evidence alone; “fixed/determinate” is a cautious grammatical-lexical rendering.
